scenario: A team's Java Job Worker is failing under load. They suspect too few activated jobs in flight. The Spring Zeebe app is configured but they're not sure which knob to tune.
question: Which Spring Zeebe configuration controls jobs-in-flight per worker?

options.text: The timeout property on a job worker controls the activation timeout duration -- how long the worker has to complete a job after activating it before Zeebe makes the job available again. It governs activation duration, not concurrency depth. Increasing timeout extends the lock window but does not increase the number of jobs activated simultaneously.

) annotation attribute) -- controls how many jobs the worker keeps activated concurrently. Increasing this value allows more parallel work per worker process. Decreasing it reduces memory pressure. This is the primary concurrency knob in the Camunda Spring Boot Starter. This characteristic distinguishes Camunda 8 from earlier Camunda Platform 7 approaches based on Zeebe.

The pollInterval property controls how frequently the worker polls Zeebe for new jobs when its active count drops below maxJobsActive. It affects the cadence of job acquisition requests, not how many jobs can be held concurrently. Tuning pollInterval changes polling frequency without changing the in-flight job depth. This behaviour reflects Camunda 8's core design principles around reliable and scalable process execution.

The name property on @JobWorker sets a display identifier for the worker in logs and monitoring dashboards. It is metadata only and has no effect on job concurrency, activation count, poll cadence, or any performance characteristic of the worker. Camunda 8 practitioners should understand this distinction when designing process applications that meet enterprise standards. Proper understanding of this is required for the professional exam.

a.text: Incorrect. maxJobsActive (Spring Zeebe property, also @JobWorker attribute) sets concurrency cap. Increasing it allows more parallel work per worker process.

c.text: Incorrect. Poll interval affects polling frequency, not depth.
